name: High Level Design

metadata.description: Defines the high-level architecture of the solution, including system boundaries, major components, integrations, data flow, deployment view, and key design decisions.

sections.title: Architecture Overview
sections.purpose: Provide a concise overview of the proposed solution architecture and how it satisfies the business, product, functional, and technical requirements.

sections.title: Architectural Drivers
sections.purpose: Describe the key requirements, constraints, quality attributes, and assumptions that drive the architecture.

sections.title: Solution Scope and Boundaries
sections.purpose: Define the boundaries of the solution, what is included within the architecture, and what remains outside the scope.

sections.title: System Context
sections.purpose: Describe how the solution interacts with users, upstream systems, downstream systems, third-party services, and enterprise platforms.

sections.title: High Level Component View
sections.purpose: Define the major application components, services, modules, or domains and explain their responsibilities.

sections.title: Integration Architecture
sections.purpose: Describe how the solution communicates with internal and external systems.

sections.title: Data Architecture
sections.purpose: Describe the high-level data model, data ownership, storage approach, and movement of data through the system.

sections.title: Security Architecture
sections.purpose: Describe the high-level security model of the solution.

sections.title: Deployment View
sections.purpose: Describe the runtime topology and the environments in which the solution will operate.

sections.title: Availability, Resilience, and Scalability
sections.purpose: Describe how the architecture addresses uptime, failure handling, recovery, and scaling expectations.

sections.title: Observability and Operational Considerations
sections.purpose: Describe the high-level approach to monitoring, logging, tracing, alerting, and supportability.

sections.title: Technology Choices and Rationale
sections.purpose: Summarize the major technology choices and the reasoning behind them.

sections.title: Architectural Risks and Trade-offs
sections.purpose: Capture key architectural risks, compromises, and trade-offs made in the proposed design.

sections.title: Open Issues and Decisions Pending
sections.purpose: List unresolved architecture questions and decisions that must be closed before implementation progresses.

sections.title: Appendices and Reference Material
sections.purpose: Capture supporting references, linked documents, and related architecture artifacts.

sections.title: Approval and Sign Off
sections.purpose: Capture formal review and approval of the high-level design.

validationRules: The HLD must align with technical requirements defined in the TRD.
Major architectural components must map to functional areas described in the FRD where applicable.
Integration architecture must identify all relevant external or enterprise interfaces.
Security architecture must align with stated security requirements.
Deployment view must not contradict the hosting or environment strategy.
Technology choices must include rationale rather than only listing tools.
Architectural risks and trade-offs must be explicitly documented.

promptHints.systemIntent: Generate a structured high level design document suitable for architecture review, delivery planning, and downstream low level design.
rules: Do not invent systems, integrations, technologies, environments, or constraints not supported by inputs.
Keep the document at architecture level and avoid detailed implementation design.
Ensure architecture decisions trace back to requirements and constraints.
State unknowns and pending decisions clearly instead of guessing.
mustDo: Use exact section titles from the template.
Explain component responsibilities and boundaries clearly.
Document trade-offs and unresolved issues honestly.
mustNotDo: Do not collapse the HLD into a low-level module breakdown.
Do not omit security, deployment, or resilience considerations.
Do not present assumptions as confirmed facts.
